text: I always check my email in the morning.
I usually reply in the morning, never at night.
I'm busy on Monday. Are you free on Tuesday?
I am never late with a deadline.
How do you spell your name?
Good evening. Welcome to Miami. How can I help you?
Hi. My flight is on Friday, and I don't know the gate. Where is the gate?
On Friday? Then nobody knows yet. The gate is on the screen, two hours before the departure. Today you just arrive — where do you sleep tonight?
I have a reservation at the hotel. How do I get there?
The hotel bus is outside, door number four. It's free. What is your name?
Paula Morales. M — O — R — A — L — E — S.
Thank you. You spell it slowly — I like that. Most people don't.
One more question. When does the flight to São Paulo arrive on Saturday?
At nine in the morning. Door number four, on your right. Have a good trip, Ms. Morales.
I have a reservation. My name is Paula Morales.

Hi, Paula. David here. Your trip to Miami is next week, and I have one thing to tell you — and it is not about the flight. The flight is on Monday. The departure from São Paulo is at ten in the morning, and the arrival in Miami is at six in the evening. The gate is on your boarding pass, but it changes — so look at the screen, always. You have a reservation at the hotel, three nights, and the office is ten minutes from there. Now the important part. This time you travel alone. People are going to ask you things: What is your name? How do you spell it? Where are you from? And you are going to want to ask things too. Here is my advice, and it is the only one I have: ask the question. Don't build a beautiful sentence in your head first. Where is the gate? Four words. That is a complete question, and it works. See you on Monday, at nine.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here, about Miami. Two things, and then go and pack. One: the work is the easy part. You know the accounts, you know the numbers, and in the Miami office everybody speaks slowly for you — I asked them to. Two, and this is the real one: the airport. You are alone, and you are going to be afraid at the airport. I know, because I was. Twenty years ago, my first day in New York: I did not ask anything. I walked for one hour, lost, because I did not want to say a wrong sentence. Don't do that, Paula. Here is the secret nobody tells you: at an airport, nobody wants a beautiful sentence. They want a question. Where is the gate? Four words, and somebody points. That is all. Ask the question, and let it be ugly. See you in Miami.
How do you spell your name?
The departure is at ten in the morning.
I have a reservation at the hotel.
I have a reservation. My name is Paula Morales.
